citation: Brett BL, Huber DL, Wild A, Nelson LD, McCrea MA. Age of First Exposure to American Football and Behavioral, Cognitive, Psychological, and Physical Outcomes in High School and Collegiate Football Players. Sports Health. 2019;Vol XX(No X).
abstract: This cross-sectional study examined the relationship between age of first exposure (AFE) to American football and behavioral, cognitive, psychological, and physical outcomes in 1,802 active high school and collegiate football players. Players were dichotomized into AFE before age 12 vs. age 12 or older, with general linear models used to assess differences across numerous clinical outcome measures. After controlling for covariates including age, concussion history, race, and ADHD diagnosis, AFE before age 12 did not significantly predict poorer performance on any clinical outcome measures (Ps >0.05). A single statistically significant association was found: AFE <12 was associated with lower somatization scores, suggesting potentially less somatic distress.
tldr: Starting football before age 12 was not associated with worse behavioral, cognitive, psychological, or physical outcomes in high school and college players; earlier starters actually reported lower somatic symptom severity, suggesting youth football participation does not impair functioning during adolescence.
methods: Cross-sectional study; N=1,802 active high school (9 schools) and collegiate (4 colleges) football players enrolled in the Project Head to Head 1 and 2 studies (August 2012–September 2017). AFE was self-reported and dichotomized at age 12. Potential covariates identified via t-tests and chi-square tests were entered into a multivariable binary logistic regression. Single-predictor general linear models (GLMs) and covariate-adjusted GLMs were performed for each clinical outcome variable including neurocognitive (ImPACT, SAC, TMT, WAIS-IV), psychological (BSI-18, BSSS, DIS-11), and physical (BESS, King-Devick) measures.
findings:
- AFE <12 vs. ≥12 did not significantly differ on any clinical outcome after covariate adjustment (all Ps >0.05 except somatization).
- AFE <12 was significantly associated with lower BSI-18 somatization scores (F=11.39, p=0.01) after covariate adjustment.
- Without covariates, AFE <12 showed significantly higher ImPACT visual memory scores (F=6.98, p<0.01).
- Covariates of age, race, ADHD, and concussion history differed significantly between AFE groups and were more strongly associated with clinical outcomes than AFE itself.
- Effect sizes for AFE on clinical outcomes were uniformly near zero (partial η² ≤ 0.005).
limitations: Cross-sectional design limits causal inference. AFE relied on athlete self-report, introducing recall bias. Other sport participation throughout development was not controlled. High correlation between AFE and total football exposure (r=0.80) makes it difficult to disentangle the two. Results may not generalize to former or older athletes where long-term sequelae may be more apparent.
practicalImplications: Current evidence does not support restricting youth football participation before age 12 based on cognitive or psychological harm during adolescence. Screening and management of ADHD, concussion history, and demographic factors are more clinically relevant predictors of outcome than age of football start. Programs focused on athlete well-being should address these modifiable health factors rather than focusing solely on AFE.
athleteDev: Directly relevant to American football player development. Findings suggest that beginning tackle football before age 12 does not impair neurocognitive or psychological development through high school and college. This supports age-appropriate youth football participation without evidence of intermediate-term harm, though long-term effects in former players remain an open question.
rtp: No direct return-to-play implications studied. However, the null finding for AFE on concussion-related outcomes (SAC, SCAT3 symptom scores) suggests that a younger start to football does not inherently elevate concussion symptom burden at baseline, which is relevant context for baseline testing and RTP protocols in young athletes.

citation: Humphreys BR, Ruseski JE. Air quality and employee performance in teams: Evidence from the NFL. Economics and Human Biology. 2023;51:101310.
abstract: This economics study examined the impact of ambient PM2.5 particulate matter concentration on NFL team performance using play-level data from approximately 90,000 offensive plays across 900 NFL games from 2008 to 2016. Air quality data from EPA monitoring stations within 10 miles of 22 NFL stadiums were linked to game-day PM2.5 readings. A reduced-form linear regression model estimated yards gained or lost per offensive play as a function of local PM2.5 concentration, controlling for game-specific and situational factors. Results indicated that higher PM2.5 concentration was associated with significantly more yards gained per passing play, suggesting that defensive team production is relatively more sensitive to poor air quality than offensive production.
tldr: Higher game-day PM2.5 air pollution increased passing yards per play in NFL games, indicating that defensive players — who must react to unscripted offensive actions — are more impaired by poor air quality than offensive players who execute predetermined plays.
methods: Retrospective observational study using play-by-play data from ArmchairAnalysis.com for NFL games 2008–2016 (n=113,782 plays; 65,421 pass plays, 48,361 rush plays across ~900 games at 22 stadiums with nearby EPA AQS monitoring stations). PM2.5 concentration was averaged over 12pm–6pm on game day. Reduced-form linear models estimated yards gained per play as a function of PM2.5, controlling for offensive/defensive team-season fixed effects, week-of-season, quarter, score difference, yards to go, yards to goal line, play number on drive, and domed stadium indicator. Robustness checks included nonlinear PM2.5 bins, outdoor-stadium subsamples with weather controls, ozone substitution, and incomplete pass exclusions.
findings:
- A one standard deviation increase in PM2.5 (~5.63 µg/m³) was associated with approximately 4.3 additional passing yards per game per team.
- PM2.5 coefficient was 0.014 yards/play overall (p<0.05), driven entirely by passing plays (0.021, p<0.05); rushing plays showed no significant association.
- Defensive teams are affected more than offensive teams by poor air quality, based on the mechanism of reactive vs. scripted play execution.
- Home team PM2.5 air quality shocks reduced home rushing yards; visiting team PM2.5 shock increased visiting team rushing yards on defense, consistent with defense being the primary channel.
- Effect sizes are small but statistically significant and robust across multiple model specifications.
limitations: Cannot directly control for coaching decisions or play-calling adjustments in response to air quality. Sample limited to 22 stadiums with nearby EPA monitoring stations, excluding 9 NFL teams. Game-day PM2.5 is measured at the nearest monitoring station, not inside the stadium, introducing measurement error. Cannot fully account for within-game variation or exact game start times. Air quality on game days is generally in healthy or moderate ranges, limiting generalizability to high-pollution scenarios.
practicalImplications: Air quality is an environmental factor that affects team athletic performance even at moderate concentration levels. For NFL and other professional teams, scheduling, venue selection, and indoor versus outdoor stadium considerations may have measurable performance implications. Staff monitoring environmental conditions on game day could inform preparation strategies, particularly for defensive personnel whose reactive performance appears most vulnerable to PM2.5 exposure.
athleteDev: Directly relevant to American football. Findings suggest that environmental air quality on game day impairs reactive decision-making and execution for defensive players more than offensive players. This distinction between scripted (offense) and reactive (defense) roles has implications for how player preparation and performance monitoring accounts for environmental stressors during the competitive season.
rtp: Not directly studied. However, the finding that even moderate ambient PM2.5 levels impair cognitive-physical performance in healthy elite athletes suggests that air quality monitoring could be a relevant environmental health consideration in return-to-activity protocols, particularly for athletes recovering from respiratory or neurological injuries.

citation: Heintz EC, Scott DP, Simms KR, Foreman JJ. Air Quality Is Predictive of Mistakes in Professional Baseball and American Football. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health. 2023;20:542.
abstract: This study examined the impact of air quality (measured by the EPA's Air Quality Index, AQI) on performance errors in Major League Baseball (MLB) and NFL quarterback performance from 1999–2020 (MLB) and 2006–2021 (NFL). Linear regression analyses used median county-level annual AQI as the independent variable of interest, predicting team errors per game (MLB), interception percentage (NFL), and total quarterback rating (QBR, NFL). AQI was a significant predictor in all three models: higher AQI (worse air quality) was associated with more errors per game in MLB, higher interception percentage, and lower QBR in the NFL, even after controlling for relevant confounders including payroll, manager/experience variables, and prior-season performance.
tldr: Worse annual air quality in the county where a professional baseball or football team plays is associated with more team errors in MLB, more interceptions by NFL quarterbacks, and lower overall quarterback performance ratings, indicating that air pollution meaningfully degrades elite cognitive-physical sport performance.
methods: Two separate OLS regression models were used. MLB sample: 632 team-season observations (1999–2020) with errors per game as the dependent variable; predictors included median annual AQI, year, manager tenure, manager age, and natural log of team payroll. NFL sample: 705 quarterback-season observations (2007–2021) for QBs starting no earlier than 1999 and playing for one team per season; dependent variables were interception percentage and QBR; predictors included cumulative AQI, year, NFL experience, prior-season performance, and games started. Air quality data sourced from EPA (epa.gov); MLB performance data from baseball-reference.com; NFL data from pro-football-reference.com.
findings:
- A one-point increase in AQI increased MLB errors by 0.000993 per game (p=0.005); teams in high-AQI counties (e.g., Phoenix) could commit 10+ more errors over a 162-game season.
- Cumulative AQI was a marginally significant positive predictor of NFL interception percentage (p=0.059) and a significant negative predictor of QBR (p=0.035).
- A 67-point worse AQI (e.g., average county vs. Phoenix/Maricopa County) was associated with an expected decrease in QBR of ~15.4 points (~0.7 SD) and ~1.27% higher interception rate (~0.4 SD).
- Team payroll and year were significant confounders in the MLB model; NFL experience and games started were significant for QB performance.
- Results are consistent with air pollution impairing both cognitive and physical performance in elite professional athletes.
limitations: Annual median AQI is a coarse measure that does not capture game-day or acute exposure fluctuations. Sample does not account for individual athlete adaptation to local air quality or acclimatization. Environmental factors such as temperature, altitude, and atmospheric pressure were not controlled. MLB analysis cannot examine cumulative individual effects due to roster turnover. Generalizability is limited to professional male athletes performing in short, intense sport contexts.
practicalImplications: Teams located in or traveling to counties with poor annual air quality may face a compounding performance disadvantage. Performance staff and medical teams should consider monitoring AQI as part of environmental preparedness, especially when games or training camps are scheduled in high-pollution areas. The cognitive demand of quarterback decision-making and fielding accuracy appear particularly vulnerable to ambient air pollution exposure.
athleteDev: Relevant to American football, specifically quarterback development and performance. Cumulative exposure to poor air quality across a career appears to degrade the high-level cognitive performance required for accurate decision-making and pass execution. Teams drafting or developing quarterbacks in high-AQI markets may face performance headwinds unrelated to talent or training quality.
rtp: Not directly studied. However, findings that even chronic moderate AQI exposure impairs elite QB cognitive performance suggest that air quality monitoring is relevant when determining readiness for return to full competition, particularly after respiratory illness or concussion, when cognitive-physical integration is already compromised.

citation: Ballmann CG, Rogers RR. American Football Headgear Impairs Visuomotor Drill Performance in Division I NCAA Football Athletes. Journal of Functional Morphology and Kinesiology. 2024;9:169.
abstract: This study investigated whether American football facemask reinforcement level and visor/eye shield type impair dynamic visuomotor drill performance in Division I NCAA football athletes. Sixteen male athletes (age 20.4±1.3 years) completed two counterbalanced repeated-measures experiments using a FITLIGHT reactive agility drill. Experiment 1 (EXP1) compared baseline (no helmet) against three facemask reinforcement levels (light, medium, heavy). Experiment 2 (EXP2) compared baseline against three visor conditions (clear, smoke-tinted, mirrored). All helmeted and all visor conditions produced significantly slower response times than baseline, with the impairment consistent across central, mid, and outer sensor positions and upper and lower sensor levels. No significant differences in response time were found between facemask reinforcement levels or between visor tint conditions.
tldr: Any American football helmet with a facemask — regardless of how reinforced it is or what type of visor is attached — significantly slows reactive visuomotor performance in Division I NCAA athletes, suggesting the helmet-facemask combination itself is the primary source of impairment rather than the specific facemask or visor type.
methods: Randomized, counterbalanced repeated-measures design with two experiments in Division I NCAA football players (n=16; offense=8, defense=6, special teams=2). EXP1: four conditions — baseline/no helmet (BL), helmet + light facemask (HL), helmet + medium facemask (HM), helmet + heavy facemask (HH) using a Schutt Vengeance Pro helmet. EXP2: four conditions — BL, helmet + clear visor (HCV; ~90% VLT), helmet + smoke visor (HSV; ~48% VLT), helmet + mirrored visor (HMV; ~28% VLT). Dynamic visuomotor test: FITLIGHT system with 10 LED sensors on 5 poles in a semi-circle (1 m radius); participants stepped to deactivate each illuminated sensor as fast as possible; 20 total responses per attempt, 3 attempts per condition with 3-min rest. Primary outcome: average response time (ms) from the single fastest attempt. Statistical analysis: 1×4 repeated-measures ANOVA with Bonferroni-Holm post-hoc; effect sizes as partial η².
findings:
- EXP1: All helmeted conditions (HL, HM, HH) produced significantly slower overall response times than BL (all p<0.001; η²=0.091); no significant differences between facemask reinforcement levels (p>0.05).
- EXP1 by sensor position: BL significantly faster than all helmeted conditions at outer, mid, and central positions; response time slower at central vs. outer and mid positions.
- EXP1 by sensor level: BL faster than all helmeted conditions at upper and lower levels (p<0.001); upper sensors responded to faster than lower sensors (p=0.041).
- EXP2: All visor conditions (HCV, HSV, HMV) significantly slower than BL (all p<0.001; η²=0.321); no significant differences between visor types (p>0.05).
- EXP2 by sensor position: BL faster than all visor conditions; interaction between condition and position was significant (p<0.001; η²=0.037).
- EXP2 by sensor level: BL faster than all visor conditions at both upper and lower levels (all p<0.001; η²=0.461).
limitations: Small sample size (n=16) limits statistical power to detect differences between helmet conditions. Experiment conducted indoors under standardized lighting, which does not replicate outdoor game-day conditions where tinted visors may provide visibility benefits. Participants were unaware of hypotheses, but performance validity testing was not employed. Study does not examine head or neck kinematics that may partially explain compensation mechanisms. All male Division I athletes — results may not generalize to other levels or female athletes.
practicalImplications: All American football headgear configurations impair reactive visuomotor performance relative to no helmet, regardless of facemask or visor type. This suggests that training drills requiring rapid visual-motor response should be conducted in full game-day headgear to habituate athletes to these impairments. Performance and safety staff should consider the visual field and reaction time constraints imposed by headgear when designing catching, route-running, and tackling drills. Coaches advocating for reduced facemask reinforcement may not achieve meaningful reaction time benefits under dynamic movement conditions.
athleteDev: Directly applicable to American football at all levels. Findings indicate that the helmet itself — not the specific facemask or visor — is the primary limiter of dynamic visuomotor performance. For athlete development, this means players should train reaction-based skills while wearing full game equipment to build appropriate visual-motor adaptations. Visor selection (tint level) does not appear to add meaningful impairment beyond the facemask combination under standardized indoor conditions.
rtp: Relevant to return-to-play protocols following concussion or ocular injury. Because all helmet configurations impair dynamic reactive visuomotor performance, progressive return-to-sport testing that includes full equipment conditions is important to accurately assess readiness. Athletes may appear capable in no-helmet baseline testing but still show functional performance deficits in game equipment, which should be accounted for in RTP decision-making.

citation: Walton SR, Kerr ZY, Powell JR, Giovanello KS, McCrea MA, Guskiewicz KM, Brett BL. An 18-Year Study of Changes in Neurocognitive Function and Associations with Repetitive Head Trauma among Former Collegiate American Football Players: A Case Series. Archives of Clinical Neuropsychology. 2025;00:1–10.
abstract: This preliminary longitudinal case series examined neurocognitive test performance changes over approximately 18 years in 31 former collegiate American football players (mean age at follow-up 38.4±1.3 years), assessed at baseline during college (T1; c.1999–2001) and at 18-year follow-up (T2; c.2016–2018). A battery of paper-and-pencil neurocognitive tests was administered at both time points: HVLT-R Immediate and Delayed Recall, FAS Verbal Fluency, Symbol Digit Modalities Test (SDMT), and Trail Making Test-B (TMT-B). Neither sport-related concussion (SR-CHx) history nor the Head Impact Exposure Estimate (HIEE) was significantly associated with changes in neurocognitive test scores over the study period. Group-level cognitive performance did not significantly change over 18 years, though individual-level variability was extremely high.
tldr: Group-level neurocognitive test performance did not significantly decline over 18 years among former collegiate football players now in early midlife, and neither concussion history nor repetitive head impact exposure was related to cognitive change scores, though the high individual variability underscores the need for larger longitudinal studies.
methods: Longitudinal case series; n=31 former collegiate American football players who participated in the original NCAA Concussion Study (T1: August 1999–January 2002) and a 15-year follow-up study (T2: July 2016–August 2018). Neurocognitive battery: HVLT-R Immediate Recall, HVLT-R Delayed Recall, FAS Verbal Fluency, SDMT, and TMT-B. Raw change scores (T2–T1) were computed alongside Cohen's d effect sizes and coefficients of variation (CoV). Repeated-measures ANCOVA models controlled for estimated premorbid ability (WTAR). Multivariable linear regression models tested associations of SR-CHx (categorical and continuous) and HIEE with change scores. Exploratory Spearman correlations examined associations between cognitive change and BSI-18 symptom domains, PSQI sleep quality, AUDIT alcohol use, DAST drug abuse, and total years of football play.
findings:
- No significant group-level changes in any neurocognitive test over 18 years (all p-values ≥ 0.06); small-to-medium effect sizes (Cohen's d: 0.18–0.41).
- Individual-level variability was very high across all measures (CoV: 244%–560%), with both improvements and declines observed across participants.
- Neither SR-CHx nor HIEE was significantly associated with change in any neurocognitive test outcome (all ps > 0.05); SR-CHx as a continuous variable approached significance for HVLT-R Immediate Recall (p=0.054).
- Higher BSI-18 Somatic symptom scores at T2 were significantly correlated with greater declines in HVLT-R Immediate Recall (ρ=−0.395, p=0.028) and SDMT (ρ=−0.380, p=0.035).
- Higher DAST scores (drug abuse screening) were significantly correlated with greater declines in HVLT-R Immediate Recall (ρ=−0.370, p=0.040).
- Over 40% of participants had HVLT-R scores ≥1.5 SD below normative means at T1, and far fewer at T2, suggesting potential practice/learning effects or true improvement.
limitations: Very small sample (n=31) substantially limits statistical power. No non-athlete control group for comparison. Self-reported concussion history and HIEE reliance introduces recall and estimation error. HIEE does not capture pre-high school participation. Cross-sectional exploratory factors (BSI-18, DAST) measured only at T2, precluding examination of concurrent changes. Performance validity measures not employed. Limited racial/ethnic and socioeconomic diversity reduces generalizability.
practicalImplications: Former collegiate football players at approximately 15+ years post-sport do not show uniform neurocognitive decline as a group, which provides some reassurance for players and staff concerned about long-term brain health. However, the wide individual variability means that population-level reassurances do not preclude meaningful decline in specific individuals. Somatic symptom burden and substance use emerged as potentially meaningful correlates of cognitive change and should be monitored and addressed in former player health programs.
athleteDev: Relevant to long-term athlete health monitoring in American football. The lack of a clear group-level cognitive decline trajectory over 18 years among former collegiate players in early midlife provides longitudinal evidence that competitive collegiate football does not universally result in measurable neurocognitive impairment by middle age. High individual variability suggests that identifying who is most at risk requires larger prospective studies accounting for lifestyle, health behaviors, and biological factors.
rtp: Not a return-to-play study; participants were 15+ years removed from collegiate play. However, the finding that somatic symptoms and substance use — rather than head trauma history alone — correlate with cognitive change scores suggests that comprehensive health monitoring protocols for retiring players should extend beyond concussion history to include mental and physical health screening as part of long-term athlete care.
